metadata.summary: Python 3.12/3.13/3.14 features (PEP 695 generics, PEP 701 f-strings, free-threading) and modern tooling (uv, ruff, hatchling, pyright). Pin one Python version per repo; keep requires-python, ruff target-version, and mypy python_version in sync.

content.title: Modern Python 2026
content.summary: Python 3.12/3.13/3.14 feature guide and tooling stack (uv, ruff, hatchling, pyright). Covers PEP 695 generics, PEP 701 f-strings, free-threaded mode (3.13t/3.14), t-strings (3.14 only), deferred annotations, and uv-based project bootstrap. Core rule: pin one Python version per repo via .python-version; keep requires-python, ruff target-version, and mypy python_version in sync.

why.p: Syntax from 3.14 (t-strings, lazy imports) pasted into a 3.12 project causes SyntaxError at import time — a silent failure that only manifests in CI or production. Consistent tooling alignment (uv + ruff + hatchling) eliminates the "works on my machine" class of environment bugs and cuts install time by 10-100x.

list.item: Bootstrapping a new Python project (uv init, pyproject.toml, ruff + mypy + pytest).
Migrating 3.10/3.11 code to 3.12+: built-in generics, PEP 695 type parameters, modern f-strings.
Replacing Black + Flake8 + isort + pyupgrade with a single Ruff config.
Setting up CI/CD with lockfile-aware caching and pinned Python versions.
Locked legacy environments (Python 3.8, RHEL system Python, vendored interpreters).
Production free-threaded mode without a thread-safety audit of all C extensions (3.13 experimental, 3.14 stabilizes).
Pure data-science notebooks where conda/mamba is entrenched.
Existing healthy poetry/PDM project — switching costs more than the benefit.

rule.statement: On Python 3.12+ floor, use PEP 695 type parameter syntax (class Box[T]:, def first[T]:(), type Alias = ...) instead of TypeVar/Generic boilerplate.
rule.rationale: PEP 695 eliminates T = TypeVar('T') declarations and the Generic[T] base class. It co-locates type parameters with their scope, removing TypeVar naming collision risks and reducing import noise.

rule.statement: Use PEP 701 f-string features on 3.12+: quote reuse inside expressions, multiline expressions, and backslashes in expressions. Do not use % formatting or .format() for new code.
rule.rationale: PEP 701 removes all f-string restrictions that made them hard to use for complex expressions. Quote reuse (f"Hello {d['key']}") eliminates the awkward workarounds that pushed developers back to .format().

example.title: PEP 701 f-string features

rule.statement: Use TypeIs[T] (PEP 742, Python 3.13+) for bidirectional type narrowing functions. Do not use TypeGuard[T] for new narrowing functions on 3.13+ floors.
rule.rationale: TypeIs narrows both the True and False branches of an if-check. TypeGuard only narrows the True branch, leaving the else side unnarrowed. TypeIs is the strict upgrade and is preferred for all new narrowing functions.

rule.statement: Do not enable free-threaded mode (PYTHON_GIL=0, python3.13t) in production without first auditing all C extensions for GIL-free thread safety. Python 3.13 free-threading is experimental.
rule.rationale: Free-threaded Python 3.13 is labeled experimental. Many C extensions assume GIL protection. Enabling free-threading without an audit causes data races in C extension code that works correctly under the GIL.

rule.statement: Use t-strings (PEP 750, Python 3.14+) for SQL queries, HTML templates, and other injection-risk string constructions. Never use f-strings for SQL or HTML — t-strings produce Template objects that renderers can sanitize.
rule.rationale: f-strings interpolate immediately, making SQL/HTML injection possible when user input is included. t-strings defer interpolation — the Template object carries the parts separately, allowing renderers to escape/parameterize before producing the final string.

rule.statement: Do not use t-strings on Python 3.12 or 3.13 codebases — they are a syntax error. Verify the repo's requires-python floor before adopting any 3.14-only syntax.
rule.rationale: Syntax errors from version-mismatched features manifest at import time, not at the specific call site. On a 3.12 project with a 3.14 CI runner, the code "works" in CI but fails in production. requires-python + ruff target-version are the guardrails.

example.title: t-string for SQL-safe query construction (Python 3.14+)
example.code:
# t-strings (Python 3.14+) — returns Template object, not str user_input = "'; DROP TABLE users; --"
# Safe: renderer sees the parts, can parameterize query = t"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = {user_input}" # db.execute(query) # DB adapter renders safely as parameterized query
# vs f-string — UNSAFE for SQL # query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = {user_input}" # SQL injection!

rule.statement: Use uv for Python version management, virtual environments, and package management. Do not mix uv with pip, venv, pyenv, or poetry in the same project.
rule.rationale: uv is 10-100x faster than pip (warm cache), manages Python versions itself, and uses a global cache. Running pip alongside uv produces environment state divergence — the lockfile (uv.lock) becomes unreliable.

rule.statement: Commit uv.lock to version control. Run uv sync --frozen in CI to install exactly what the lockfile specifies.
rule.rationale: uv.lock is the reproducibility contract. --frozen fails if the lockfile is stale, catching accidental uv add commits that weren't also followed by uv lock. This prevents "works in CI, breaks in prod" dependency drift.

example.title: Essential uv commands
example.code:
# Init uv init my-project --python 3.12 uv python pin 3.12 # Creates .python-version
# Deps uv add fastapi pydantic uv add --dev pytest ruff mypy pytest-cov
# Run (no venv activation needed) uv run python main.py uv run pytest --cov=src uv run ruff check . --fix && uv run ruff format .
# CI uv sync --frozen # Install exact lockfile state

rule.statement: Enable ruff rule groups: E F I B UP N S C4 PT. Run ruff check --fix before commit and ruff format in pre-commit hooks. Use target-version matching requires-python.
rule.rationale: Ruff replaces Black + Flake8 + isort + pyupgrade in a single millisecond-speed pass. UP auto-upgrades deprecated typing syntax. S catches security-relevant patterns (hardcoded secrets, subprocess shell=True). PT enforces pytest style.

example.title: Ruff config in pyproject.toml
example.code:
[tool.ruff] line-length = 100 target-version = "py312"
[tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E", "F", "I", "B", "UP", "N", "S", "C4", "PT", "T20"] ignore = ["S101"] # assert allowed in tests
[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores] "tests/**" = ["S"] # Security rules relaxed in tests
[tool.ruff.format] quote-style = "double"

rule.statement: Use hatchling as the build backend for new libraries and distributable packages. For application-only projects (no PyPI distribution), the build backend can be omitted.
rule.rationale: hatchling is the PEP 517/518 build backend recommended by the Python Packaging Authority (PyPA). It replaces setuptools for new projects and integrates cleanly with uv. It requires no setup.py or MANIFEST.in.

rule.statement: Keep requires-python, ruff target-version, mypy python_version, and pyright pythonVersion in sync. All four must reference the same minor version.
rule.rationale: Misalignment produces false CI passes: ruff may not flag syntax errors from a higher version if target-version is too low; mypy may allow features not available on the deployment Python if python_version is too high. Sync prevents silent version drift.

rule.statement: Run ruff check --select UP --fix as the first step of any 3.12+ migration. This auto-migrates all deprecated typing imports (from typing import List → list, Optional[X] → X | None) and other pyupgrade fixes.
rule.rationale: The UP rule group covers the mechanical 3.12+ migration items that need no judgment: built-in generics, union syntax, removed aliases. Auto-fixing first eliminates noise so manual review focuses on structural changes.

rule.statement: Migrate TypeVar/Generic classes to PEP 695 syntax when touching a file for another reason. Do not create a dedicated "PEP 695 migration" PR — it produces large diffs with low value per change.
rule.rationale: Opportunistic migration keeps the codebase moving forward without a big-bang rewrite that blocks other work. "Touch a file, modernize it" is a sustainable practice that fully migrates the codebase over time.

example.title: TypeVar to PEP 695 migration target
example.code:
# Before (3.11 and earlier) from typing import TypeVar, Generic
T = TypeVar('T') K = TypeVar('K') V = TypeVar('V')
class Cache(Generic[K, V]): def get(self, key: K) -> V | None: ... def set(self, key: K, value: V) -> None: ...
# After (3.12+) class Cache[K, V]: def get(self, key: K) -> V | None: ... def set(self, key: K, value: V) -> None: ...

rule.statement: When switching a poetry project to uv: export requirements.txt from poetry, then uv add each dependency. Do not manually copy pyproject.toml dependency lists — version constraint syntax differs.
rule.rationale: Poetry uses ^ and ~ constraints that do not translate directly to uv's PEP 440 syntax. Exporting requirements and re-adding via uv ensures the lockfile is generated fresh from the actual resolution, not inherited from a different resolver.

rule.statement: Replace Black and Flake8 with ruff in pre-commit hooks in one commit. Keep the existing line-length setting to avoid a massive reformatting diff.
rule.rationale: Switching formatters with a different line length produces a diff that touches every file, making git blame useless for recent history. Keeping the existing line length in the ruff config produces a minimal diff from the formatter switch.

template.purpose: Full pyproject.toml: uv, hatchling, ruff (E F I B UP N S C4 PT), mypy strict, pytest-asyncio auto mode.
One-shot project bootstrap: uv init, add deps, pre-commit with ruff + mypy hooks.
